The agent has read Algorithm 1 and understood the information it presents.
The plan includes train initial model f on full dataset D (or load a pretrained classifier).
The plan includes partition dataset into forget set D_F, retain set D_R, and test set D_T according to the target forget percentage.

The plan includes for each sample x in D_F, generate adversarial example x_adv using PGD-50 attack with L2 norm bound; start with a small epsilon and double it until the model prediction is different from the original label.
The plan includes assign a random wrong label y_wrong (any class other than the ground truth) to each x_adv to form the adversarial dataset D_A.
The plan includes (Optional, for AMUN+SalUn) Compute weight saliency mask on f by gradient-based importance estimation using D_F to restrict parameter updates.
The plan includes assemble fine-tuning dataset: if D_R is available use D_R ∪ D_F ∪ D_A; if only D_F is accessible use D_F ∪ D_A; for very large D_F, use D_R ∪ D_A or only D_A.

The plan includes fine-tune f on the assembled dataset for 10 epochs using cross-entropy loss and a learning rate chosen by grid search over [10^{-6}, 10^{-1}]; apply saliency mask from step-5 for AMUN+SalUn.
The plan includes evaluate unlearned model on D_F, D_R, D_T to compute Unlearn Accuracy, Retain Accuracy, and Test Accuracy.
The plan includes run membership inference attack (RMIA) on the unlearned model to compute FT AUC (or MIS for legacy comparisons) between D_F and D_T.
The plan includes compute Average Gap as the mean absolute difference between the unlearned model and the Retrain baseline for Unlearn Acc, Retain Acc, Test Acc, and FT AUC (or MIS).
The plan includes the setting 'with remain set': Fine-tune on D_R ∪ D_F ∪ D_A when D_R is available; for very large D_F, use D_R ∪ D_A.
The plan includes the setting 'without remain set': Fine-tune on D_F ∪ D_A when only D_F is accessible; for very large D_F, use only D_A.

The plan includes the setting 'AMUN+SalUn': AMUN combined with weight saliency masking from SalUn to restrict parameter changes during fine-tuning, applicable in both with- and without-remain settings.
The plan includes the use of the CIFAR-10 dataset.
The plan includes the use of the Tiny Imagenet dataset.
The plan includes the use of the ResNet-18 model.
The plan includes the use of the VGG19 model.
The plan includes the use of the Adversarially trained ResNet-18 (TRADES) model.
The plan includes the use of the Clipped ResNet-18 (Lipschitz-regularized) model.
The plan includes the Retrain baseline for comparison.
The plan includes the FT baseline for comparison.
The plan includes the RL baseline for comparison.
The plan includes the GA baseline for comparison.
The plan includes the BS baseline for comparison.
The plan includes the l1-Sparse baseline for comparison.
The plan includes the SalUn baseline for comparison.
The plan includes the Unlearn Accuracy evaluation metric.
The plan includes the Retain Accuracy evaluation metric.
The plan includes the Test Accuracy evaluation metric.
The plan includes the FT AUC evaluation metric.
The plan includes the MIS evaluation metric.
The plan includes the Average Gap evaluation metric.
The plan specifies the hyperparameter 'Adversarial attack method' with value PGD-50 with ℓ₂ norm bound.
The plan specifies the hyperparameter 'Initial epsilon (ϵ_init)' with value small (unspecified); doubled until adversarial example found.
The plan specifies the hyperparameter 'PGD step size' with value 0.1 × ϵ.
The plan specifies the hyperparameter 'Fine-tuning epochs' with value 10.
The plan specifies the hyperparameter 'Learning rate' with value grid search in [10⁻⁶, 10⁻¹].

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: AMUN achieves the lowest Average Gap, outperforming all baselines when the remain set D_R is available during unlearning (CIFAR-10, 10% and 50% forget sizes).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: AMUN dominates prior methods when only the forget set D_F is available, and AMUN+SalUn further improves the Average Gap (CIFAR-10, 10% and 50% forget sizes).

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 2: AMUN outperforms other methods in continuous unlearning (multiple sequential requests) by maintaining a larger gap between D_R vs D_F and D_F vs D_T AUCs.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: AMUN and AMUN+SalUn outperform baselines on Tiny Imagenet (VGG19) when D_R is accessible.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 5: AMUN and AMUN+SalUn significantly outperform baselines on Tiny Imagenet when only D_F is accessible.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: AMUN remains effective on adversarially robust (clipped) ResNet-18 models even without access to D_R; FT AUC stays near random guessing.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 6: AMUN is effective on clipped robust models when D_R is available.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 7: AMUN is effective on adversarially trained ResNet-18 models (TRADES) when D_R is available.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 1: Using adversarial examples with wrong labels (D_A) preserves test accuracy during fine-tuning; replacing D_A with random-location or random-label datasets leads to accuracy degradation, especially without D_R.

The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 1): AMUN achieves the smallest Average Gap among approximate methods when D_R is available, significantly closing the gap to retrained models.

The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 2): Without access to D_R, AMUN (and AMUN+SalUn) substantially outperforms other methods, while other methods fail (GA degrades, RL/BS/SalUn produce near-perfect accuracy but poor MIA scores).

The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Table 1): After unlearning 10% of CIFAR-10 with AMUN, the SOTA membership inference attack RMIA cannot distinguish forget samples from test samples (AUC ≈ 50%).
The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Figure 1): Fine-tuning on adversarial examples does not cause catastrophic forgetting of test accuracy.

The execution outcomes match the specific claim (based on Figure 2): AMUN handles continuous unlearning requests effectively; after 5 sequential unlearnings of 2% each, the gap between D_R vs D_F and D_F vs D_T AUCs remains large (better than baselines).
